name: Capstone
classification_notes: Capstone draws on classic Roman letterforms with stylish low-contrast serifs, placing it in a transitional zone between oldstyle and display serifs with a vintage ephemera character.
summary: A seven-weight low-contrast serif drawing on classic Roman letterforms and the vernacular of early to mid-20th century packaging and ephemera.
description: Designed by Jason Carne, Capstone spans from Hairline to Heavy across seven weights, making it useful for both delicate captions and bold headline work. The low-contrast serifs carry a vintage warmth rooted in early 20th century packaging and print ephemera rather than formal typography traditions. Each style ships with over 400 glyphs including small caps, supporting 75 or more Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: Carmel Type Co. describe Capstone as a seven-weight workhorse inspired by classic Roman letterforms and the low-contrast serifs found in vintage ephemera and packaging from the early to mid 20th century. The lighter weights are intended for footnotes and captions, while the heavier weights are built for commanding headlines and bold display use.

name: Botanist
classification_notes: Botanist is a Victorian-inspired display serif with sharp serifs, flowing curves and decorative capital alternates, placing it firmly in the ornate display category rather than text use.
summary: A Victorian-inspired display serif with sharp serifs, flowing curves and a set of decorative capital alternates designed for ornamental use.
description: Designed by Jason Carne, Botanist draws on Victorian typographic tradition with crisp, razor-edged serifs and delicate curved strokes. The standard uppercase is restrained enough for formal settings, while the accompanying decorative capitals add flourish suited to packaging, branding or editorial display work. The family covers 380+ glyphs with support for over 75 Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: Carmel Type Co. describe Botanist as a Victorian-inspired delight plucked from the gardens of elegance, featuring razor sharp serifs and flowing delicate curves. The standard capitals carry a sense of class and tradition, and when paired with the thoughtfully crafted decorative capitals, the family fully comes into its own.

name: Deathspell
classification_notes: Rooted in a revival of Hohenzollern, a transitional letter style bridging blackletter and art nouveau, placing it firmly in the blackletter category with a distinctive transitional subcategory.
summary: A revival of Carl Albert Fahrenwaldt's Hohenzollern script, expanded across nine weights with support for over 75 Latin-based languages.
description: Based on the Hohenzollern letter style first published in the 1909 Schriften Atlas, this face occupies a distinctive territory between traditional blackletter and art nouveau. Jason Carne extended the original 26-character specimen into a full working family with 380+ glyphs per style, custom diacritics, currency symbols and punctuation across nine weights. The result is a century-old aesthetic made practically usable for contemporary display work.
foundry_blurb: Carmel Type describe Deathspell as a devilish hybrid, beginning as a revival of Fahrenwaldt's Hohenzollern before expanding into a fully modern typeface family. The original specimen offered only basic characters, so the design was broadened to include 75+ language support, custom diacritical marks, and nine distinct weights totalling over 3,400 glyphs.

name: Skyward
classification_notes: Skyward is a geometric display typeface with Art Deco sensibilities, designed primarily for headlines, packaging and branding rather than continuous text. Its tall proportions and ornamental refinement place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A tall, geometrically refined display typeface by Jason Carne with Art Deco sensibilities, offered in sans, serif and rounded variants across eight faces.
description: Carrying over 300 glyphs across eight styles, Skyward splits into three distinct subfamilies: sans serif, serif and rounded, each sharing the same upright, deco-inflected proportions. An alternative character set extends the typographic options for packaging, window signage and magazine headlines. The family is positioned squarely at luxury branding and bold advertising where a vertical, considered geometry is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Carmel Type Co. describe Skyward as robust, towering and geometrically refined, calling it a classic cocktail of utility and elegance with Deco-era sensibilities and modern refinement. They recommend it for luxury products and packaging, bold ad campaigns, window signage, magazine headlines and branding projects, noting it works best as display text.

name: Heist
classification_notes: Heist is a display serif derived from the 'Blackface Bank-Note Roman' tradition, characterised by extreme stroke contrast, triangular serifs and heavy weight, historically used on American currency and postage stamps.
summary: A modern reinterpretation of the 'Blackface Bank-Note Roman' tradition, drawing directly from Edward M. Weeks's engraved letterforms that appeared on American currency and postage stamps.
description: Jason Carne reworked Weeks's original engraved blackface roman, softening some triangular serifs and adding weight to the lighter strokes to reduce the most extreme contrast points of the source material. The family ships in three variants, Regular, Inline and Shadow, with over 400 glyphs covering punctuation, fractions, six currency symbols, catchwords and diacritical marks for 75+ Latin-based languages. Best suited to heavy headline, packaging and print work where the weight and historical register of American currency lettering is the point.
foundry_blurb: Carmel Type Co. describe Heist as a modern re-interpretation of the primary letter style that has graced American paper currency and postage stamps for well over a century, inspired by the original 'Blackface Bank-Note Roman' engraved by Edward M. Weeks. They note that previous digital recreations of this style have not matched its scope, citing Heist's 400+ glyphs, full European Latin language support, catchwords, fractions and multiple currency symbols as distinguishing features.

name: Ironbound Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed sans-serif with strong retro-display character, drawing from mid-century neon signage and cast metal storefront lettering, placing it firmly in display rather than text use.
summary: A nine-weight condensed sans inspired by mid-century neon motel signage in Wildwood, New Jersey and the cast metal letters of classic New York City storefronts.
description: Designed by Jason Carne, Ironbound Condensed draws from the doo-wop motel strip of Wildwood, New Jersey and the disappearing delicatessens and diners of New York City. Each of the nine weights ships with around 700 glyphs, including small capitals, fractions, superiors, inferiors and coverage for over 75 Latin-based languages. The result is a display typeface with clear period references that works well for signage, packaging and branding with a nostalgic American character.
foundry_blurb: Carmel Type Co. describe Ironbound Condensed as a nostalgic sans for a new day, with designer Jason Carne citing mid-century neon signage from Wildwood, New Jersey and the cast metal storefront letters of classic New York City establishments as his primary inspirations. The family spans nine weights with approximately 700 glyphs each, supporting over 75 Latin-based languages and including small caps, fractions and a full punctuation set.

name: Alchemist
classification_notes: A semi-condensed, high-contrast Roman display face with ornate, sign-painting influences. Its decorative character and primary use case in headlines and titles places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A semi-condensed, high-contrast display serif by Jason Carne, drawing on ornate sign-painting and lettering traditions for headlines and cinematic titling.
description: Inspired by early sign-painting and lettering books as well as natural forms, this display face pairs classic Roman proportions with decorative detail and strong contrast. The family ships with over 100 alternate characters and more than 40 ligatures across a 500+ character set, giving designers room to vary compositions considerably. Suited to signage, packaging, gaming and cinematic titles where ornate weight and visual drama are needed.
foundry_blurb: Carmel Type Co. describe Alchemist as inspired by nature, the elements and the decorative alphabets of early sign-painting and lettering books. They position it as a semi-condensed, high-contrast, stylish take on classic Roman forms with a 500+ character set including over 100 alternates and 40+ ligatures, intended as a go-to staple for signage, headlines, cinematic and gaming titles and packaging design.
